data.title: Allison Transmission: Not A Great Year And 2026 May Not Be Very Different
data.description:
Introduction

It has been over a year since the first time I covered Allison Transmission (ALSN), and now that we have the full year’s results, it is a good time as any to revisit. Since the first article, when I gave it a hold rating, due to an uncertain macroeconomic outlook, the company’s share price advanced 16.6% vs SPY’s (SPY) 11.5%.

The trucking demand remains soft in key markets, with some positives in other segments; however, not enough to offset the negatives fully. Therefore, my initial hold rating stands.

Briefly on Performance

Now that we have the full year under our belt, let’s take a look at the top-line performance. Revenue for FY25 was around $3B, which is down almost 7% y/y. The same issue is still causing the company’s top-line performance to be lackluster. The NA on-highway demand, especially medium-duty and vocational truck builds, is causing it. On the positive side, the defense segment and outside NA sales helped offset the issues slightly.

Going over the company’s margin development in 2025, we can see a mixed bag. Overall, gross profitability stayed strong, but its operating expenses and product mix moved quite a bit over the year. Most of the margins went down at the end of the year, apart from gross margins, which essentially were maintained. Pricing held up pretty well, with contractual passthroughs helping the company maintain and even improve gross margins.

With the decline in the bottom line, we naturally get a decline in other efficiency metrics like ROA and ROE. These are still quite attractive, but they have declined quite a bit from the beginning of the year. Also, the company’s return on total capital took a hit, which is not ideal. The company may be losing its pricing power, and its moat is eroding.

Going over to the company’s balance sheet, ALSN finished the year with around $1.5B in cash and equivalents, against close to $2.9B in long-term debt. In terms of liquidity and solvency, the debt on ALSN’s books is not an issue at all. The company’s interest coverage ratio was around 10x. Its operations can easily cover the current debt obligations. Additionally, the company continues to generate a great amount of cash flow. Cash from operations came in at around $836m, while free cash flow was around $661m, giving the company a free cash flow margin of around 22%. This is quite strong for an industrial company. The prior year’s free cash flow was closer to 17%, so that is quite an improvement. The important part of this is whether ALSN can maintain this level of cash generation in the future.

In my view, 2025 was not the best year for ALSN. Profitability decreased, while revenues continued to suffer due to the NA market’s weakness. All key efficiency metrics took a massive hit because of it, and it doesn’t look like we will see any recovery just yet.

Comments on the Outlook

Guidance for 2026

Net sales are going to be in the range of $5.575B-$5.925B. This is a massive jump, but it is artificial because the company recently closed an acquisition of Dana’s Off-highway segment, which added around $2.5B in sales. This business is going to generate sales in the range of $2.55B-$2.75B.

On the EBITDA guidance, the company set a range of $1.365B-$1.515B, or $1.44B at the midpoint. Sales expectations were lower, but analysts expected EBITDA guidance to be around $1.53B. So, that was disappointing. Overall, I don’t see anything impressive here.

Diversification lever

So, we know that the company has been struggling to gain traction due to the weakness in the NA on-highway market. The other segments are just not big enough to help diversify this concentration risk. Additionally, the weakness is lasting longer than anyone expected. The question is, what is the company doing to help diversify its operations? We saw a big hint in the previous section. Yes, the company decided it wants to add an off-highway revenue segment to its portfolio. On-highway appears to be a lot more cyclical, so diversifying away from this truck market is a very good sign. ALSN wants more exposure to off-highway, industrial, and electrified drivetrains.

Off-highway revenues are expected to be around $2.5B for the year. We don’t have the numbers for 2025, but ALSN did provide us with some performance numbers for 2024. Here, we can see that net sales were around $2.7B, down from $3.085B. We can safely say that the off-highway business addition will be decreasing as well. Or it will be flat, given the range ALSN provided. So, off-highway seems to be struggling too. When should we expect these markets to begin recovering? This is a tough question indeed. Many expected it to improve last year, but the tariff fiasco made everything so distorted that no one wants to transport anything, especially not at these prices. With the oil prices skyrocketing due to geopolitical worries, I don’t see how off-highway and on-highway demand is going to improve right now. Trucking operators are the ones who will feel it first due to worse fuel economics.

What we will probably see that will offset some of the downturn again is the increase in defense spending during these trying times. I don’t think it is going to be enough, but any cushion is positive.

The Defense Angle

Even though the defense segment is still relatively small, it has become a lot more relevant to the whole operation. The more growth this segment sees in the future, the higher the margins will get for the company overall, as these contracts tend to be rather lucrative. The segment is also less cyclical than commercial truck demand and, as we saw, helped offset some of that weakness. In 2025, Allison won an $81m Abrams Tank contract and then another $97m Abrams -related contract, helping revenue reach $267m for 2025. The key product in these contracts is the X1100 cross-drive transmission, along with some kits, overhaul, and other technical support. This seems like a long-term recurring revenue relationship rather than a one-off component sale. And that is an attractive business to many investors.

There is no way that the tailwinds of the defense segment are going to help the company to be seen in a better light, given how badly the other segments are performing, but it does help its overall story of diversification. I’m going to be paying attention to this segment a lot more to see how its growth progresses over the next few quarters.

Valuation

For valuation, I went ahead with what analysts were expecting for the first two years, and then the rest of the assumptions will be anchored there. Below are these assumptions.

We can see revenue will surge by 91% due to the acquisition, and then I don’t think there will be anything exciting. If anything, I may be too optimistic by assuming there will be growth in the first place. For margins, I am also assuming there will be accretion since there should be some synergies and cost savings, as well as the change in mix. The rest of the model, I’m taking the company’s historical averages (more or less). Additionally, I am taking a 10% discount, which is close to the company’s WACC, and I’m using 2.5% for my terminal growth rate. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we come to the following intrinsic value for ALSN.

Investor Takeaway

So, it seems the company is trading close to its fair value currently, and given that I am not particularly excited about the company’s core operations right now, I am maintaining a hold rating and wouldn’t look to add to a position if you already own it. There isn’t a lot of growth to be had just yet, and I would like to see how the addition of the off-highway segment is going to affect ALSN’s overall operations.

Additionally, the defense segment seems to be more interesting, but too small to pay too much attention to. The macroeconomic outlook is still quite jittery, so I wouldn’t be surprised if we see some more corrections ahead.

data.title: QQQI With JEPQ: Outperforming QQQ Amidst The 2026 Tech Correction
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) are two hybrid funds designed to generate double-digit dividend yields whilst maintaining exposure to the growth of stocks included in the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX). In view of the specific nature of the funds’ strategies, a pressing issue is the existence of a "growth ceiling," as a result of which actual returns lag behind the index during periods of active market growth. But 2026 has been marked by a period of heightened market volatility, leading to a decline in the sector’s value and showing signs of a fairly deep technical correction (NDX has fallen by as much as 13.1% since the high of the year). In the context of such market conditions, such funds as QQQI and JEPQ demonstrate more favorable results, mitigating the decline compared to the benchmark—Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ). At the beginning of 2026, total return performance for QQQI and JEPQ was 1.52% and 3.06% better, which confirms their high investment appeal during a period of geopolitical instability and rising inflation risks.

My article's thesis centers on investing in the QQQI and JEPQ funds, both of which have a competitive advantage over the QQQ benchmark. Both funds offer a conservative approach to investing in the Nasdaq-100, offsetting potential drawdowns by generating returns from the premiums of sold options. Even if external circumstances beyond our control and difficult to predict continue to put pressure on these U.S. sectors, a portfolio of QQQI and JEPQ will allow us to partially offset negative price action by continuing to generate double-digit dividend yields. As these funds maintain their exposure to the growth of stocks in these sectors, it allows us to lock in a significant portion of the profits that will emerge from a potential recovery of the Nasdaq-100. In this way, a bet on QQQI and JEPQ allows us to be less dependent on the market phase itself, whilst maintaining various avenues for generating returns.

Forecast Thesis on the Nasdaq-100

This thesis is based on the expectation that the total return profile of QQQI and JEPQ would be more stable than that of QQQ. And this forecast thesis is based on an analysis of the current market conditions of the Nasdaq-100. There is an increase in market volatility, due to the persistence of geopolitical risks stemming from the lack of de-escalation in the Middle East conflict.

Firstly, one should take into account that the VIX index is showing extremely high values, remaining above the 20–22 range since early March 2026 and periodically rising above 30. This index's rise is driven by volatility, enabling QQQI and JEPQ to generate higher premiums from selling options, thereby increasing the funds' overall returns.

Secondly, if the conflict in the Middle East continues, I expect the NDX correction to potentially deepen to 20% (its current maximum drawdown is 13.1%). Unless the situation de-escalates by April 2026, the energy market could impact the economic environment, triggering a new wave of selling in that sector (for example, the Fed could begin signaling tighter monetary policy due to rising inflation risks).

Thirdly, should the NDX extend its correction, an additional 7-10% decline in that sector would result in an automatic loss for the QQQ over the same range. That said, both QQQI and JEPQ employ specific strategies designed to minimize drawdowns, thereby positively impacting their overall return profiles. QQQI, for example, has the ability to generate a more aggressive option premium, because further correction would trigger a rebound in the VIX to the 30 level. That would automatically increase profits from the option strategy, partly offsetting the loss in value of the underlying assets. JEPQ can use its strategy to increase the share of stocks with a lower beta and, similarly, to reduce its drawdown through premium growth by actively managing options. In the end, NDX drawdown is accompanied by increased volatility, the most important component driving the growth in the total returns of QQQI and JEPQ compared to QQQ.

Fundamental Similarities Between QQQI and JEPQ

Because QQQI and JEPQ are both hybrid ETFs designed to generate high cash flow with exposure to growth stocks, the two funds share many fundamental similarities.

  • Both funds are benchmarked to the Nasdaq-100, with large-cap stocks from that sector serving as their underlying assets.
  • When necessary, fund managers may employ active dynamic selection as part of their management strategy, enabling them to deviate from the exact index composition to maximize returns and minimize risks.
  • Revenue is generated through the use of an options strategy, in which selling volatility is the primary condition for earning a premium.
  • Under their dividend policy, funds aim to achieve double-digit returns, allowing for monthly distributions.
  • The two funds offer a balance between capital growth and protection, creating a "growth ceiling" but mitigating volatility if the market is in a sideways or bearish trend.

The Fundamental Concept of QQQI

QQQI's focus is on tracking the Nasdaq-100, with the majority of its investments in the Technology sector (50.17% of the total portfolio) and the Communication sector (15.62% of the total portfolio). Mag-7 companies are included in the top 10 holdings. This portfolio consists of 104 holdings, in which the top 10 assets account for 46.68%. By tracking the index, performance is smoothed out, although the potential to generate alpha from the growth of individual stocks is reduced.

Current QQQI dividend yield stands at 15.54%, with the dividend itself having been reduced in February 2025 from $0.64 per share to $0.61 per share. Nevertheless, dividend dynamics remain fairly stable overall, considering that fund managers use an options strategy to generate income. The goal of QQQI is to strike a balance between payouts and capital preservation in order to generate double-digit yield without eroding NAV.

QQQI option strategies are based on Nasdaq-100 index spreads (where options are sold in conjunction with call spreads). Fund managers purchase additional call options to complement the options they have already written, which allows them to generate a premium on the spread. An advantage of this option strategy is the tax-free return of capital, since the options fall under Section 1256. According to Form 8937 for 2025, the share of capital returned in the payout structure was 98.95%.

The Fundamental Concept of JEPQ

JEPQ, like QQQI, tracking the Nasdaq-100 index, uses a different instrument for this purpose—ELNs. They are contracts with JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM) as the counterparty, which allow you to mimic the returns from selling call options. The disadvantages of this approach include counterparty risk (even though I don’t believe JP Morgan Chase will default) and an additional fee, but overall this instrument is effective in practice. This allows JEPQ to generate a double-digit yield of 11.10%, even though the payouts themselves have been quite volatile over the past year (they range from $0.44 to $0.62). Also, the payouts themselves are classified as ordinary income.

Portfolio JEPQ consists of 108 holdings, where the top 10 account for 40.40% of the portfolio. This lower concentration of assets compared to the QQQI is because managers have more flexibility in determining the weighting of individual assets. When stocks appear overbought, the portfolio's allocation to them is reduced, with the freed-up capital reallocated to stocks with greater growth potential. Also, a difference is that the portfolio is not 100% hedged, only the dynamic part, that depends on the market phase. When the bullish market potential is higher, there is less hedging, or vice versa. By using this approach, managers of JEPQ partially offset the "growth ceiling" problem.

Advantages of QQQI and JEPQ

This is especially evident when the market is in a sideways or bearish trend. This ability to generate returns through an options strategy creates a "safety cushion" which partially offsets a decline in the index. Approximate returns from options amount to 10–12% per year, allowing for a monthly offset of up to 1% of the index’s decline. Even when the market is in a sideways trend, funds still generate returns through the time decay of options. As such, in a sideways market, QQQI and JEPQ can demonstrate better results compared to the QQQ benchmark. In addition, if the price of the fund’s shares falls, the investors can reinvest dividends at more favorable levels, thereby increasing the effectiveness of position cost averaging. For the long term, that significantly increases future cash flow from payouts.

As a comparison of ETFs, I suggest referring to the following table.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

The main risk associated with the funds under review relates to potential erosion of NAV if the market enters a phase of a sharp downtrend. Historical performance data for QQQI and JEPQ do not provide sufficient insight into how resilient the funds’ capital is during periods of severe market declines. Considering a 20-30% collapse of the Nasdaq-100 over 6-12 months, the funds will definitely face NAV erosion under such conditions. It will lead to a potential reduction in their payouts and a more difficult capital recovery once the market returns to a phase of steady growth. Another thing to keep in mind is that QQQI and JEPQ are conservative instruments for maintaining exposure to those U.S. sectors, but they are not a complete hedge against a decline in the Nasdaq-100.

Conclusion

I would conclude that QQQI and JEPQ warrant a "Buy" rating, which means they can be considered as part of a personal investment portfolio. The purchase of these funds allows you to maintain a bet on a potential recovery of the Nasdaq-100, but at the same time receive monthly payments compared to investments in the QQQ benchmark. Currently, with high market volatility in these sectors, QQQI and JEPQ are funds that can capitalize on these conditions in the form of higher premiums from options strategies. This is because the higher the market volatility, desto more expensive the options themselves become—and consequently, so do the profits. These funds are therefore an excellent way to find a temporary safe haven amid market uncertainty whilst maintaining double-digit dividend yields.

data.title: Newmont: A Falling Golden Knife Worth Catching Now (Upgrade)
data.description:
Newmont: Finally, A Buying Opportunity To Capitalize

As you are reading this write-up, I guess you probably know that gold mining stocks have been trying to form a bottom for the past couple of weeks. Buyers are stubbornly refuting the selling pressure that threatened to deepen the reversal of the gains accumulated for 2026. At one point, most, if not all, the gains for gold (GLD) were reversed, which also affected gold mining leader Newmont Corporation (NEM).

Precious metals, especially the alluring yellow metal, have been one of the hottest properties for the past year. And for those that mine them, such as Newmont, these are certainly moments to savor and enjoy. Reaping in the benefits linked to high operating leverage in their capital expenditure heavy portfolio, which obviously is a boon in such dominant times for the gold bugs.

So, I think there isn't any shadow of doubt that gold mining investors are relishing this, and probably couldn’t believe that the market is still pricing them at a relative discount when juxtaposed against their long-term averages. And for NEM's 11.5x, it even appears that the market hasn’t quite gotten FOMO over it, especially when paired against that of Barrick Mining’s (B) 10.9x multiple, as well as Agnico Eagle’s (AEM) markedly higher 15x forward multiple.

In other words, I think one could argue that NEM’s multiples might even have potential for a further rerating if it could deliver on bringing down the costs of its portfolio of assets. At the same time, while also lifting its sales volume higher, in line with management’s long-term outlook of hitting 6 million ounces in production.

Yet, I also highlighted in my last update that NEM doesn’t quite sit on the lower end of that AISC cost curve. And obviously, the issue of renewed inflationary pressure on mining has become more prevalent recently due to the ongoing Iranian conflict, and how they can drive up oil prices and pile onto other costs in the value chain.

This is something that Newmont also cautioned in its 10-K, in which their “operations and projects may be adversely affected by rising energy prices or energy shortages.”

Higher Costs Still A Bugbear To Deal With

So, I think the market is clearly trying to price in these uncertainties, especially since a higher inflationary environment might require the Fed to raise interest rates (although far from certain at this point). In addition, a higher inflationary environment could cause NEM’s AISC to continue going higher, of which costs are already anticipated to rise for 2026, of which NEM’s cost structure is clearly not the cheapest, and neither is it the lowest in terms of year over year growth.

So I guess some skepticism has likely been reflected quickly in the market, as NEM fell 30% from its 2025 peak before the recent bottom. I didn’t quite expect the de-rating to happen so quickly, as the external threats, higher inflationary dynamics pressuring gold prices and also plausibly lifting AISC, could have wreaked havoc in investor psyche.

Yet, I don't think dip buyers are complaining, especially since the market appears to have given patient investors a fantastic opportunity to consider doubling down right now, as the gold market and precious metals in general appear to be forming a very nice bottom.

And what’s important is that I think the company has already laid out the projects to not only help secure future higher production, but also is expected to see these flow through to the bottom line before the end of the decade, as seen with the series of scheduled completions from H2 2027 to 2029.

Of course we will need gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) to stay elevated, of which the current uncertainties in the Middle East appear to have dampened sentiments lately. However, with structural bullish drivers largely intact, and coupled with worsening geopolitical tensions not likely to be lowered anytime soon, I don’t quite see how this level of pessimism in gold mining stocks will persist.

And that probably suggests that high free cash flow margins should be the norm through the late 2020s, although the crucial question is whether there could be an intervening downcycle normalization before the next advancing phase could ensue.

Always remember that Newmont is a mining company, and they need to delicately manage and balance their risk/reward, while increasing production at the right time to benefit from the price surges.

But, as a price taker, they don’t quite have control over the gold pricing cycle, right? What they could achieve is managing their cost curve and portfolio or asset base optimization, which I guess NEM has been executing diligently. Yet, the outlook doesn't indicate that NEM falls into the lower half of the cost outlook, and the AISC outlook for 2026 remains relatively on the high side as compared to peers, as discussed earlier.

Therefore, these uncertainties could continue to stymie a more pronounced valuation re-rating. And, it could be further hounded by a return to assessed midcycle pricing, which Morningstar has also emphasized in a recent update.

Margins Expected To Stay Elevated

Nevertheless, NEM’s gross margins are still generally on the high side, lending credence to its ability to manage costs effectively. Free cash flow margins are also roughly in line with the leading peers in the market. And NEM’s 5Y FCF average also supports the conviction needed that the company has performed relatively well through the cycle.

However, the risk of a prolonged downcycle cannot be ruled out, especially since the current FCF margins are already so much higher than what NEM and peers have achieved over the past five years. Is there really much more room for further upside?

In other words, I think the most significant risk right now is a return to a much higher inflationary scenario that could really dampen and hit investing sentiment hard on NEM. I guess that's why it appears to justify why the market has been somewhat lukewarm on its valuation multiple. You get what I mean, right?

Buy Or Sell NEM Stock?

While these concerns are generally valid, I still want to refer to and infer the motivations of dip buyers who decided to pick up the falling knives over the past 3 weeks.

As we can see from the chart above, NEM's $100 level has held resolutely, affirming the support zone since December of last year. So, I think this goes to show that buying sentiments have remained robust at the "right" levels, where accumulation is clearly gleaned.

I'm not saying that the $100 level is not impenetrable, but it at least gives us conviction that buyers are defending that line very hard and very stoutly. And these buyers aren't willing to give it up this quickly (and easily), weighing the risk that runaway inflation shouldn't be the base case right now.

Therefore, it parlays well into the narrative that NEM should be able to manage the production increases constructively through the rest of the decade, helping it to raise sales volume over time in tune with these output expansions.

That should help to keep FCF margins at a relatively high level to bolster multiples expansion subsequently. Moreover, with NEM’s forward P/E of 11.5x well below the 5Y average of 16.6x, I think the message is clear.

The market is already well cognizant of the risks of a potential earnings and FCF downcycle that investors wouldn’t want to be caught unaware and unprepared for if the midcycle price assumptions were to follow through earlier than expected.

We already know that central banks were pressured to sell their holdings, aligning with the steep selloff in gold ETFs observed over the past month or so.

Therefore, investors shouldn’t simply assume that even these rational investors wouldn’t adjust their calculus when needed, when more important (and urgent) imperatives come into play.

In this case, the ambiguities alluded to in the ME conflict will continue to dominate our risk matrix and are unlikely to be resolved anytime soon.

That said, I think the current risk/reward context and price action have broadly accounted for the enunciated risks, making me a lot more confident to return to the bullish side of the ledger for NEM.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Falcon's Beyond - Peeling The Onion
data.description:
Falcon's Beyond (FBYD) develops amusement parks and other entertainment platforms. The company states to have three divisions: 1) Falcon's Beyond Destinations - theme parks, hotels and attractions, 2) Falcon's Beyond Brands - proprietary digital games and consumer products, and 3) Falcon's Creative Group - master planning services for themed entertainment. But most of these divisions' operations are shared investments, accounted for as equity in earnings. Only Falcon's Beyond Brands and a small part of Falcon's Beyond Destinations are consolidated operating businesses.

For 2025, Falcon's Beyond reported consolidated revenues of $14.9M and an operating loss of ($13.4M). Net operating cash outflow was ($24.6M). Below the operating line, the company reported non-cash equity-in earnings of $17.0M, but would have been ($13.0M) excluding a non-recurring investee's Tenerife property sale of $30.0M. Net loss from continuing operations attributable to common shareholders was ($28.3M) versus reported net income of $2.8M that included one-off property sales.

For Q4 2025, the company reported revenues of $6.6M, an operating loss of ($2.0M), and a net loss of ($0.3M).

To the company's credit, 2025 showed some improvement over 2024. Revenues jumped from $6.7M to $14.9M and operating loss narrowed from ($15.9M) to ($13.4M). But net operating cash outflow worsened dramatically from ($12.6M) to ($24.6M).

But the big elephant in the room is the huge overvaluation of $1.47B or 99x revenues. Furthermore, the company's multi-layered capital structure of debt, preferred stock, and Common B stock usurps and/or dilutes most of the value from the publicly-traded Common A shares.

The company has 48.2M Common A shares. In addition, there are Common B shares of 47.9M, convertible Preferred B shares (with an 11% coupon) of 6.7M, and warrants that convert into 1.3M common shares - a total of 104.1M shares. At the current price of $14.10, FBYD has a market cap of $1.47B or 99x revenues.

The company has financial debt of $15.6M and cash of only $1.9M. The company has an $18.1M liability for two lawsuit claims (one case for $7M remaining to be paid within 10 months, and another case pending where the company has set aside an accrual for $11.1M, see recent 10-K footnote 12). Then the company has preferred shares with a liquidation value of $33.6M that pays an 11% coupon. Finally, there are the Class B common shares held by insiders (that are convertible into A). This leaves very little residual value for the quoted common A shares.

Falcon's Beyond derived 45% of 2025 revenues from accounting, legal, human resources, information technology and other support services to the Falcon's Creative Group of which it is a shared owner (see recent 10-K, page 11). These semi-circular revenues to a related entity are labor-intensive sales. Also, in its first partial year of operations, Falcon’s Attractions (part of the consolidated Falcon's Beyond Brands division) generated 28% of 2025 revenue from product sales and services to a themed entertainment operator at a 44% gross margin. These loss-making service revenues or standard product sales (even with a decent 44% gross margin) normally carry a revenue multiple of below 1x, not 99x.

The company had a very rosy presentation (see March 30th press release) of its results that had contributed to a surging stock price recently. Over the last week, FBYD shares had more than doubled from $6.47 to $14.10, while the stock market had been tanking. The bull case may be the improved revenues and narrowed operating losses in 2025 and Q4 versus the prior year. Possibly headlining the larger revenue numbers of the unconsolidated equity affiliates in the news release may have excited investors.

What was obscured was the fact that these equity affiliate revenues are below the operating line, and normally are not categorized as revenues. Furthermore, the bulk of these equity affiliate revenues are from Falcon's Creative Group, which derives 99% of its revenues from only two Saudi Arabian customers (QIC 60% and NMDC 39%, see 10-K, page 11) - and QIC is a 25% owner of Falcon's Creative Group.

Moreover, as mentioned in the previous paragraph above, 45% of consolidated revenues came from Falcon's Creatiive Group, and Falcon's Beyond is a shared owner of Falcon's Creative Group. Thus, Falcon's Beyond is generating 45% of its revenues from a company in which it is a major owner.

When you peel away the onion, there is very little here to justify anything close to a $1.47B market cap in my opinion. Even the exaggerated revenue numbers that include equity affiliate revenues cannot get close to supporting FBYD's bloated market cap. To make matters worse, the company is hemorrhaging cash and its capital structure leaves little value and solvency risk for common A shareholders in my view.

Short interest is fairly low at 2.5%, which is attractive for an extremely overvalued stock like FBYD. The borrow rate is currently around 50%, which is 3x higher than a few weeks ago, but I have seen worse borrow rates for these types of stocks.

FBYD is a compelling fundamental short.

data.title: Invesco: Lowered Expectations For 2026E (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This is going to be another article where I focus on precise valuations and targets. Because the target of this article, Invesco Ltd. (IVZ), saw significant surges in share price following my article, touching close to $30/share in a very short time from the article that I published, which can be found here.

In that article, I maintained Buy with a price target ("PT") of $24/share, which was actually breached between the time of submission and publishing, if you look at where it's recorded.

While you would have outperformed the market even holding to that Buy rating, the magic of valuation comes when you act in accordance with market movements - not just static long-term targets. Of course. I would never (or very rarely, if there was a reason for it), sell a qualitative company at a price just cents above my PT. But the situation is different when that same company moves almost 25-30% in a very short time. At that scenario, I have alerts set on every company I own.

In this case, the price alert was set first at $27, then at $29/share, both of which were breached at separate times. When it hit $27/share, I did not act. When it hit $29/share in mid-January, I decided to sell my stake (barring a small watchlist position, which I retain to this day). I managed to eke out a very nice double-digit RoR, which of course came to triple-digit from an annualized perspective. At the time, I didn't really look back from a results-based analysis - because the reason for selling was simply that the company moved to a short-term price that I relative to fair value viewed as being unsustainable.

In this article, I intend to give you my valuation update for IVZ, and show you why I'm somewhat more conservative about the company today.

However, let me first share with you my rationale for selling, and why I viewed prices approaching $30/share as far too high for comfort.

Invesco: Why the company wasn't attractive at $30/share or close, and what we have for 2026E

The surge in the company's share price was part of a longer-term outperformance, in fact of a 50%+ surge in a very short time. This not only set off alarm bells with me, but other analysts as well, because unless the company is suddenly worth a lot higher premium, such a move can indicate, relative to traditional valuation, overvaluation.

At $30/share or close to it, I believe the company is interpreting far too lightly the industry fee and margin headwind as well as competition. Fund investing has become every asset manager's game, and the differentiation has grown less meaningful, which puts certain pressure on management fees. Some analysts that I follow, such as TD Cowen, have in fact increased their PT's for the company to between $30-$36/share for optimism, but I find this to be far too optimistic for what the company offers investors.

And the thing is, I believe I was right. I believe the prompt "down" performance on the part of the company was very clear proof that the valuation, at least for the short-term, was considered at too high a level.

Now, however, we have new results and it's been over 5 months since my last article. We're in April of 2026, and it's a good time to look at expectations for IVZ. It's trading not below $24/share, but above, which means I'd need to upgrade my PT to consider this one a Buy. Given my relatively bearish view of the market, and the crowded market of asset managers and fund managers, I view that to be a tall order, however.

We start with recent earnings - Q4 '25, which were released about 2 months back. 2025 was a good year for the company. We still saw EPS improvements, OI improvement, and lower OpEx increases than profit increases, which goes to the company's operating leverage. The fact that leverage decreased to 2.2x, while the company managed to reduce its headcount by almost 1,000 people is impressive in itself. The company deserves high marks for this. Most of the company's multi-asset and overall funds performed above the benchmark, with significant amounts in the higher quartiles - the worst performers were fundamental equities.

Inflows remained solid, and revenues increased over 8%.

So far, all good. I looked for signs of fundamental or operational weaknesses, and at first glance, there are very few.

That is good - and that is why I had the company at $24/share PT, which was actually fairly high in valuation compared to some. Based on what some investors argue, the surge up to $30/share or close to it, merely represents the market getting "too excited" for a moment, before cooling down - with the values and potential still very much being there, if we look at growth. After all, Invesco is BBB+ rated, it has very conservative fundamentals (as evidenced above), it has a decent yield, if one below treasury (3.46%).

So what are the problems?

Well, as I said in my previous article, the correlation to broader market performance is a hard one to break. It goes without saying that I am more bearish now than I was in November 2025 on the market as a whole. This has to do with the shape of AI, with the shape of software, but generally, that I view the market as overvalued. Because so much of Invesco's returns are tied to directionality in the market, as we can see from its historical returns and accuracy, this introduces new sources or at least, increased potential of risk.

On the surface, this sort of expectation tells a very compelling and positive story. I can understand why many investors see this and want to invest. However, I argue that it doesn't tell the whole story. It doesn't tell us that on a 1-year basis with a 10% margin of error, Invesco typically (more than 50% of the time) misses its estimates negatively. It almost never outperforms. It is, in fact, a very unstable company that over time has underperformed the SPY more than it has outperformed.

Really, the only period where this company has outperformed the broader market is the last 1-year period. Every other comp shows a negative alpha relative to the SPY (meaning lower returns). This tells me a couple of things. It tells me not to try to accurately forecast the company for the long term, because it is akin to trying to forecast the broader market - futility. It tells me to look at where the company is well-priced and well-valued, and focus on that - and also to react in a scenario where the company "shoots up," because this can, as you can see both recently, and historically, happen.

My problem, therefore, with Invesco isn't fundamentals or management. It's because in a market decline, a growth-oriented player like Invesco (with QQQ and the like), always underperforms due to exposure to high-beta. This includes AI and tech. Ergo, when and if this turns down - Invesco will turn down more. Ergo, if you, like me, are uncertain about the potential of these markets and their profitability, it's not the time to go into a fund manager with exposure to high-beta assets.

This is doubly the case because as a growth fund, or as growth-oriented players, its approximate valuations are incredibly sensitive to assumptions of forward earnings - which themselves are directly tied to changes in interest rates. This means that interest rate change potentials, which we are currently looking at, are also likely to "correct" this.

I argue, in fact, that this is the entire reason the company saw such a quick and sharp correction as it did. Invesco itself, in fact, said that its funds underperformed in Q4 '25 specifically because of how they were weighted.

This dictates to me, dear readers, that we should be careful.

Valuation for Invesco - Not as good as expected

You may have already sensed from the way that I argue or discuss, that I will be lowering my PT for Invesco. This is the case. In the case of continued growth, Invesco without a doubt has a very solid upside. But it's my conviction that the market is far more likely to enter correction or negative territory - and for that reason, I am not positive on the future few years, and as a result I don't believe the company will grow as expected either. Current estimates are for a 15-18% annualized AEPS growth until 2028E. I believe this is predicated on a very positive outlook for tech and AI, which I in turn consider to be unlikely. I am therefore halving the growth rate. Mind you, this still comes to an impressive 8-9% AEPS growth per year until 2028E, and a distant AEPS of $2.55 compared to $2.03 in 2025. That, to me, is still a tall order.

But it's more realistic. However, at the 10-11x P/E average, this comes to less than 6% per year in capital appreciation, and with an assumed dividend at similar levels to today, even with high growth, below 10.5% per year. Even if I were to allow a higher P/E for the company, owing to its 13-15x P/E premium and go to 12x, the company does not manage to go above 12-13% annualized, even with the dividend included.

I therefore consider my previous PT to be inaccurate, and in need of update. I now work from an 11.5x forward P/E with an assumption of 6-9% AEPS growth, which comes to a Pt of about $21/share. With the assumed growth in dividends, at $21/share or below, the company would achieve over 15% annualized for investors even with more conservative assumptions.

I find myself unlikely to buy even at this price, though. Given the current convictions I hold for the market, I believe I would prefer investing in less correlated companies with better long-term performance. The scenario where I would have interest in Invesco is in a significant market crash, where this company can be scooped up at very low prices. In such scenarios, this company has proven that it can outperform.

Risks are now as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The main argument against investing here and taking that risk would be market movements or a stretched valuation relative to what could be expected. Remember also that Invesco cut its dividend once, as I have covered previously, and may very well do so again. As I mentioned above, only half of its AUM is outperforming peers on a 3-5Y basis. The result of this can typically be an outflow of AUM over time, especially when passive investing outperforms. This speaks to the high beta that I have covered in this article.

I'd also like to emphasize a general market downturn and the risks associated with it. In a significant crash, which I believe is coming (when is uncertain, however), there tends to be a fair bit of outflow, and this will impact Invesco as it impacts others.

My updated thesis for 2026E is therefore as follows.

Thesis

This company is a good but not class-leading investment/asset manager. It yields a nice 3.4%+, and further dividend cuts seem extremely unlikely at this point, given recent trends. Credit is solid despite the BBB+, and I see no fundamental issues in the near term any longer. However, the recent surge and drop and the current state of the market speak to an increased risk profile. I therefore recalculate the company's future potential, and move to a Hold with a PT of $21/share, downgraded from $24/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $21/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Micron Stock: Why I'm Doubling Down
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc.'s (MU) wild ride has continued the last few weeks. Shares spiked to all-time highs leading up to a highly anticipated earnings report, only to fall precipitously the last few days. There are a few headwinds coalescing simultaneously that are causing this downturn, but I think the market reaction is overblown and that the stock will get back to its march higher in short order.

In my last piece on MU back in late January, I discussed how the memory maker was grappling with massive demand, why this shortage has been particularly pronounced, and the outsized profits that were cresting the horizon. The stock rose 15% over the next two months before crashing back down, now 20%+ off highs. That article can be read here.

This was just the most recent in a long series of articles that I've written on MU since initiating a Strong Buy in September 2024 (+286% since) and then downgrading to a Buy on November 2025 at around $200 per share, which I've maintained.

Throughout this run, I have seen many folks claim that the cyclical era of memory is over, that structural demand from AI will buoy the industry, and that "this time is different." I have even seen more than a couple references to Micron being "the next Nvidia." However, I have been adamant in my opinion that, despite the explosion in revenue and profit for Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung (SSNLF), memory remains as cyclical as ever. And my reasoning is simple: memory is cyclical because the markets it supplies are cyclical.

Smartphones, PCs, and processors all fall into this category. And yet, these products weren't always cyclical -- when they first burst onto the scene, the demand ceiling seemed impossibly high. Then, of course, competitors entered, technology became commoditized, and before anyone saw it coming, the market became oversupplied and prices crashed. So, the claim that AI isn't cyclical is myopic -- AI isn't cyclical because it hasn't been around long enough to see a downcycle. But that doesn't mean one isn't coming.

This may sound like semantics, but I think it's important to distinguish the difference because there are many investors who are piling into MU right now to catch the next NVDA, not realizing that, as has happened many times before, the stock is always in danger of a steep decline caused by a demand shock. Fixed costs in memory production are substantial and capacity expansion takes years to plan and complete, meaning Micron and its South Korean peers are about as far from mobile and agile as a company can be.

Therefore, I would posit that investing in Micron isn't about debating whether the memory manufacturing business is still cyclical -- it's about figuring out when the upcycle will turn into a downcycle. With that context now top of mind, let's jump into the company's most recent earnings report:

Going into the report, the market was expecting fireworks and Micron certainly delivered. Revenue of $23.9 billion was up 196% YoY and an outrageous 75% QoQ, gross margin ballooned to 74.9%, an increase of more than 1800 bps, and non-GAAP EPS smashed the company's record and came in at $12.20, 155% higher than the previous quarter.

While the memory shortages were driven by high bandwidth memory, or HBM, demand for AI data center build-outs, the ripples were felt across the industry at large, leading to an interesting dynamic:

While HBM revenues (counted within the Cloud Memory division) surged and gross margin got a boost to 74%, the difference is "only" about 800 bps QoQ. Looking at the company's other segments reveals even greater margin gains in the non-AI markets, with Core Data Center (OEM data center customers) matching the 74% gross margin for AI sales, and Mobile and Client (consumer division) exceeding that number by an additional 500 bps. Even Automotive and Embedded saw a substantial increase.

So HBM is driving the shortage, but from a volume perspective, the increases in DRAM and NAND prices are providing even greater benefits to Micron's non-AI business. Which makes sense -- HBM customers were already paying top dollar so the magnitude of the price changes is lower while products like DDR4, for example, are dirt-cheap to produce and are now selling for extremely elevated prices.

To capture some of the excess demand, Micron and the South Korean memory makers are picking up spending as the former committed $5 billion to capex in Q2 (while still managing nearly $7 billion in adjusted free cash flow). But as the shortage worsens, the Big 3 have remained slow to ramp capacity because decades of booms and busts have taught that the optimal path is to make gradual, measured moves to increase supply, even if demand reaches a fever pitch.

This wait-and-see approach will allow the industry to achieve a balance between supply and demand without crashing prices and risking another bust so soon after the previous one. And it will also allow them to make a mind-boggling amount of money. Micron provided the following guidance:

The company expects revenue of $33.5 billion at the midpoint (+260% YoY/+40% QoQ), gross margin around 81%, and non-GAAP EPS of $19.15 at the midpoint, which is +57% QoQ. To project these figures after what was already a historic quarter for Micron demonstrates just how dire this shortage is and just how persist demand has remained despite the higher prices. A gross margin of 81% is higher than Nvidia's (NVDA)! It's hard to imagine that just a few years ago (2023), Micron reported a negative gross margin in three consecutive quarters.

Okay, this earnings report just about covers the bull case for MU -- at the current run rate, the company might just earn $60 per share over the next four quarters. That's a P/E of about 6 by the way.

And yet, even with these results, investors opted to sell the news and the stock dropped by more than 8% the following trading day as profit taking overrode operating results. The selloff worsened following the outbreak of war in the Persian Gulf, leading to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, skyrocketing oil prices, and a flight to defensive stocks. As the market has moved towards de-risking, MU has been hit by yet more selling pressure as high-beta names are being shed in favor of safer ports.

Beyond macroeconomic pressure, there have been some negative headlines the last week or so, mainly regarding Google's (GOOG) new quantization algorithm TurboQuant, which claims to speed up the key-value ("KV") cache on AI processors by orders of magnitude. As of right now, an overwhelming majority of the memory on an AI processor is used for 1) the KV cache and 2) model weights, so the idea is that if the KV cache gets more efficient, chipmakers will need less memory to meet customer needs. In response, DRAM prices have fallen more than 5% this week as the market speculates on what this could mean for future demand (though the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and inflationary fears probably have something to do with the decline as well).

So, is this improvement in memory performance going to reduce overall bit demand and hurt Micron? To answer that question, we can look to real world examples. The obvious one is the origin of what's known as the Jevons paradox. In the second half of the 19th century, famed Scottish inventor James Watt invented a highly efficient steam engine, which greatly reduced the amount of coal necessary to achieve a given output of energy. Conventional wisdom at the time was that, as efficiency rose, coal consumption would therefore drop. However, the exact opposite occurred -- coal became cheaper and more accessible, which actually boosted overall demand long-term.

This concept is known as induced demand, which essentially means that, as the cost of a resource goes down, consumption goes up. A similar example was observed with the Katy Freeway, which stretches from western Texas all the way to Houston in eastern Texas. The state added new lanes aimed at reducing congestion in the early 2000s, but after a short-term decline, traffic became equal to or even worse than before the project.

Memory is certain to be in high demand for years to come as the hyperscalers have committed to hundreds of billions in AI capex. Cheaper memory won't convince them to be satisfied with their current plans -- it will induce even further demand as they all race to win the AI arms race. So, no, I don't think TurboQuant is a negative catalyst for MU, and I expect that we might even look back on this as a moment of democratization for memory that allows other players besides the largest tech companies, which currently account for nearly all of HBM shipments, to enter the game.

Investor Takeaway

Full disclosure, I was writing this article Tuesday night before the ~10% move higher on Wednesday but my thesis remains unchanged. I expect MU to continue to outperform in the short and medium-term as the current upcycle continues and even accelerates. Capacity expansion will continue to incrementally add to revenue and net income in the quarters ahead, even as a likely easing of sky-high prices and lower utilization (of new fabs) brings gross margins down from the 80% expected next quarter.

In the long-term, I expect MU to become the victim of another downcycle eventually as AI becomes cyclical like all the rest of the secular tech trends that came before it. I don't think this inflection is approaching any time soon, but owning this stock is an exercise in always keeping your ears and eyes open for the next shock. But for now, I like MU at current price levels and expect continued movement higher as the supercharged operating results come in and tensions in the Middle East ease.

As such, I am reiterating a Buy on MU.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: 10% Yield Worth Considering From Rithm Capital
data.description:
Rithm Capital (RITM) is a mortgage REIT that is more complex than many of its peers, but it has also delivered significantly better returns over long time periods. Its business model has simply worked very well. I own shares of the common stock and some of the preferred shares; specifically, I have positions in RITM-D (RITM.PR.D), RITM-E (RITM.PR.E), and RITM-F (RITM.PR.F) for the preferred shares.

The Preferred Share RITM-A (RITM.PR.A)

When I look at RITM-A, I see a share that is trading just under our "Buy" rating. Shares were trading at $24.88 when I prepared this article.

That results in about $0.42 of dividend accrual and puts the stripped price at about $24.46. The shares have an annualized yield to call of 35%.

For RITM-A, the attractive feature is the floating yield. Based on the current short-term rates, we see a floating yield a bit over 10%. That looks pretty good. That is a high enough yield that many investors would be fairly happy with the shares.

I am not expecting an immediate call, so we're not going to focus overwhelmingly on the yield to call. Instead, we will look at the floating yield on price. RITM-A, as of writing this article, had a floating yield on price of 10.10%. These shares are trading a bit under our buy range. I am not overwhelmingly excited about them, given there are other options right now, but I'm also certainly not in a bearish position at all. I would say this is a viable price for investors who want to initiate positions, though it falls short of being a no-brainer price.

When we think about shares like RITM-A, we aim to evaluate the floating spread of 5.802% plus the short-term secured funding rate. It's typical to still discuss it as being LIBOR, since for the purpose of these contracts, they are using SOFR plus 0.26161% as a replacement for three-month LIBOR. The original contracts all referenced three-month LIBOR.

Your Expectations

  • If you believe that short-term rates will remain near current levels, or if you believe they will increase modestly, then these shares would probably be quite attractive to you.

  • If you expected short-term rates to fall under 1%, then these shares would be significantly less attractive.

  • If you expected short-term rates to fall that far within the next few years, you would want to be focusing much more on utilizing bonds or using some fixed-rate preferred shares that trade at significant discounts to call value because of a lower coupon rate. You wouldn't want shares that trade at a significant discount to call value because the credit quality is garbage. That would not be a good choice if you were projecting the short-term rates to fall.

I am also using the standard logic that you would not expect the economy to be extremely strong with short-term rates plunging. Those don't usually go together.

Federal Reserve

The Federal Reserve has demonstrated a preference for higher rates. When inflation is not below their target, this has been demonstrated in speeches by referring to the increase in the interest rate on excess reserves as normalization. They called it normalization for years while preparing to raise rates and while raising rates. However, for the larger part of a decade (around 2009 to late 2016), the rate was about 0%. That was also the entire history for the interest rate on excess reserves at the time. In my opinion, if something was approximately 0% from the time it was created until eight years after it was created, it is not reasonable to refer to rapid increases as normalization. That is abusing the English language. You can't normalize something by making it materially different from every value it has ever held in the past.

Conclusion

Now, when we think about RITM-A, it's reasonable to say that the yield to call and stripped yield make these shares attractive. RITM-A’s price would need to climb a material amount for shares to have a negative yield to call. We see some preferred shares that trade at a negative yield to call. However, it is not particularly common. At the time that I am preparing this article, there were exactly three mortgage REIT preferred shares that had a negative yield to call. Those were NLY-F (NLY.PR.F) and DX-C (DX.PR.C). Therefore, I would not expect the returns on RITM-A to be materially higher than the stripped yield or the floating yield on price. However, that suggests that it would not be unreasonable for investors to see an annualized rate of return around 10%.

There is always a risk of the price dropping further, but the risk-adjusted return profile appears attractive. I don't believe there is a strong case for prices to plunge. That generally has not happened previously. We've seen some weakness during Liberation Day. There was a great deal of weakness during the pandemic. You may recall the entire market had a great deal of weakness during the pandemic. Therefore, I think it's reasonable to base our assessments on the other periods. Outside those two events, we haven't seen significant volatility.

data.title: The Small-Cap Rotation Is Here: How Income Investors Can Participate Through SCAP
data.description:
After years of playing second fiddle to mega-cap technology stocks, small-cap equities have staged a decisive breakout. The Russell 2000 Index* (RTY) surged 5.4% in January 2026, roughly quadrupling the S&P 500* Index’s (SP500) 1.4% gain over the same period.1 That January rally included a historic 14-session winning streak for the small-cap benchmark, the longest in nearly 30 years. Market observers have dubbed the shift the “Great Rotation,” and a growing body of evidence suggests it has further room to run.

For income-focused investors, the question is not whether to pay attention to small caps. The question is how to access this opportunity while still generating meaningful yield. The InfraCap Small Cap Income ETF (SCAP) offers one answer.

Why Small Caps, Why Now

The fundamental case for small-cap stocks rests on three pillars: valuation, monetary policy, and earnings momentum. On valuation alone, small caps trade at nearly a 31% discount to large caps on a forward price-to-earnings basis.2 That gap represents one of the widest valuation spreads in recent memory and has drawn comparisons to the early 2000s, when small-cap value dramatically outperformed following the dot-com bust.

The Federal Reserve’s rate cuts to 3.50–3.75% have lowered borrowing costs for smaller companies, which tend to carry more variable-rate debt than their larger peers.3 Falling short-term rates have also stabilized net interest margins for regional banks, a sector heavily represented across small-cap indices.

Earnings results have reinforced the narrative. Small-cap companies posted a 65% beat rate in Q4 2025.4 Franklin Templeton noted that “both small-cap quality and value are poised for meaningful rebounds in 2026.”5 Morningstar strategist Dave Sekera added that, based on current valuations, small caps “have further to run.”6

Inside SCAP: Active Small-Cap Income

The InfraCap Small Cap Income ETF launched in December 2023 with a straightforward mandate: deliver income and growth opportunities from an actively managed portfolio of approximately 80 small-cap stocks.7 The fund invests at least 80% of its assets in small-cap equity securities selected through a combination of quantitative screening and qualitative fundamental analysis, with a deliberate tilt toward value stocks that exhibit both income potential and earnings growth.

SCAP’s distribution history tells a compelling story. The fund has raised its quarterly per-share payout consistently since inception, climbing from $0.175 to $0.240. That most recent increase, a $0.035 jump in December 2025, brought the annualized distribution to $2.88 per share and the 30-day SEC yield to approximately 7.07% (as of 12/31/2025).

The fund’s top holdings reflect its value-and-income orientation. Preferred Bank (PFBC), StoneX Group (SNEX), Bank of N.T. Butterfield (NTB), and East West Bancorp (EWBC) anchor the portfolio with meaningful exposure to regional financials. NorthWestern Energy (NWE) and Chord Energy (CHRD) provide utility and energy sector diversification. No single position exceeds 3.6% of the portfolio, and the overall P/E ratio stands at 16.55, consistent with a disciplined value approach.

Performance metrics support the strategy. SCAP has delivered a 15.84% annualized return since inception (12/11/2023) and a 9.94% one-year return as of early March 2026, with a year-to-date gain of 4.54%. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The investment returns and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Combining Income and Small-Cap Exposure

The traditional knock against small-cap investing has been volatility without income. Most small-cap ETFs offer negligible yield, forcing income-seeking investors to choose between growth potential and cash flow. SCAP’s structure attempts to resolve that tension by pairing active stock selection in the small-cap universe with a distribution policy designed to grow over time.

This matters especially in the current environment. With small caps trading at a historic discount, rate policy turning favorable, and earnings trends strengthening, the asset class presents a window of opportunity. Investors who have historically avoided small caps due to a lack of income now have a vehicle designed to provide both.

The rotation into smaller companies may prove to be more than a momentary shift. If valuation gaps continue to compress and economic conditions remain supportive, portfolios with meaningful small-cap exposure could benefit for quarters to come. SCAP positions itself at the intersection of that opportunity and the need for dependable monthly income.

Notes

1. HCR Wealth Advisors, January 2026 Market Review; Clark Capital Management Group, January 2026 Benchmark Review.

2. Morningstar via MarketWatch, Four Reasons to Buy This Overlooked Group of Stocks, January 14, 2026; Easterly Funds, 2026 Outlook: Small Cap Value.

3. Federal Reserve Board, FOMC Statement, January 28, 2026; Federal Reserve, FOMC Minutes, December 10, 2025.

4. Bloomberg Intelligence, Small Caps Flourish as U.S. Earnings Growth Broadens Beyond Tech, February 2026.

5. Franklin Templeton (Royce Investment Partners), What’s Next for US Small-Caps in 2026, January 2026.

6. Morningstar, The Best ETFs to Ride the Small-Cap Stock Rally, Dave Sekera, January 29, 2026.

7. InfraCap Advisors, SCAP Fund Overview and Fact Sheet; StockAnalysis.com, SCAP ETF Overview.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Certain information contained in this document constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or other comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

Original Source: Infrastructure Capital Advisors

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Snapchat: Activist Calls For 'Year Of Efficiency' (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Snap Inc. (SNAP) has been the worst performer in the social media sector. AI has helped the stronger get stronger, furthering the gap to the smaller operators like SNAP. The fundamental picture isn’t exactly pretty here, but the balance sheet is reasonable and the valuation is deeply distressed. The involvement of an activist investor might be the spark needed to shift the focus towards profitability, which I expect to be the main catalyst to hope for. I upgrade the stock to a Strong Buy rating.

SNAP Stock Price

I last covered SNAP in January, where I stuck by my Buy rating in spite of the deteriorating fundamentals. The stock has been demolished since.

In hindsight, the “low valuation” argument clearly did not offer much in the way of downside support. Call it conviction, call it stubbornness, but I remain of the view that this is no time to give up in spite of the hazy outlook ahead.

SNAP Stock Key Metrics

SNAP is a social media company that is widely known for being popular with younger generations. At one point, many (including yours truly) viewed it to be a potential “Facebook-killer.” In other words, SNAP was like TikTok before that ever existed.

There is some irony in that the present state of affairs suggests something very different, in which both Facebook and TikTok are still dominant but SNAP is now the one in danger. In the most recent quarter, the company saw global daily active users decline sequentially. The only other time this occurred was all the way back in 2018 - at the time, this was attributed to a poorly received app redesign. That ended up to be a fixable issue but this time it might not be so clear given the likely competitive drivers behind it. SNAP saw its user losses primarily in North America, which is particularly concerning given that this is its most lucrative user base.

SNAP managed to offset the DAU weakness with growth in average revenue per user ("ARPU"), but I view user growth to be a more sustainable driver of long term trends.

The company ended up posting 10% YoY revenue growth. This is a solid result given the stock valuation, but pales in comparison to the far higher growth rate at Meta Platforms (META) in spite of the latter operating at a far larger scale.

There are some green shoots. The company has seen much of its revenue growth come from higher quality sources including its subscription offering, which drove more than 50% of the revenue growth in the quarter. That said, investors probably are not looking at this using a “glass half full” kind of mentality and are instead more focused on the lower nominal growth rate.

The company made some progress on profitability, with GAAP net income increasing slightly to $45 million, representing a 3% margin.

While that margin paled in comparison to the 50% margin seen at META’s social media businesses, it represented solid progress. The company generated a 48% GAAP incremental operating margin in the quarter and 45% incremental margin for the full year. Again, the company has lots of progress that it needs to make but these are solid gains given that the company is likely to be a beneficiary from AI efficiencies over the long term.

SNAP ended the quarter with $2.9 billion in cash versus $3.5 billion of debt. I note that the company has refinanced their convertible debt using “normal” debt and thus is now paying considerably more interest expense than in prior years.

Looking ahead, management guided the first quarter to see up to $1.53 billion in revenue, representing an acceleration to 12% YoY growth. Management notably has included more verbiage regarding goals towards profitability.

On the conference call, management noted their goal to “strike a better balance between the pace of community growth and the rate of top line growth” as they shift towards profitable growth. Management did notably discuss their belief that gross margins may move higher from here as a result of intentional changes in investment philosophy. Management estimated that “something like 40% of new code at Snap is AI generated” and I again note my expectations for the company to be able to improve their cost structure through AI. Social media stocks have been in the headlines as of late due to a landmark addiction ruling. Their conference call occurred prior to that ruling, but management got ahead of the trial by noting that the “global ad revenue from impressions served to users under the age of 18 … is not material.”

Is SNAP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SNAP has seen a recovery as of late, spurred on by reports that the activist investor Irenic has taken a stake. In their letter, Irenic makes a set of suggestions that include but are not limited to aggressively boosting profitability through headcount reductions and aggressive discipline on the spectacles development. Irenic goes on to give a potential price target of $26.37 per share in the bullish case.

I find that price target to be rather aggressive, but in general agree with the recommended steps. I should note that SNAP has all the typical markets of a clear activist candidate and I wouldn’t be surprised if other activists join suit. The stock recently traded at just 9x this year’s earnings. Of course some may point out that these are non-GAAP earnings estimates (recall that the GAAP net margin remains negative).

But that misses the overarching point. SNAP trades at just around 1x this year’s sales. That is a huge discount to the roughly 6x multiple at META (and I view the latter to be deeply undervalued).

Even if we assume that SNAP’s long term net margin opportunity is significantly lower than the current 50% GAAP operating margin at META’s advertising businesses, then SNAP stock still looks too cheap. Assuming just a 20% long term net margin, SNAP is trading right around 5.5x long term earnings. The balance sheet isn’t in too bad a shape, and with AI continuing to make advances, it is quite believable to think that SNAP could drive profit margins sharply higher if and when management chooses to do so.

At this point, my own price target stands at around 2x sales, implying 10x long term earnings. This is much lower than my prior target of 3x sales and that reduction is due to SNAP’s worsening competitive positioning relative to META. I expect that competitive positioning to continue to worsen given META’s ability to invest aggressively in AI. Even so, 2x sales implies strong upside for the stock.

The only question is that of the catalyst. The only catalyst I see is that of driving profit margins higher - this is direct and likely to immediately drive that multiple expansion, but investors are at the mercy of management. Co-founders CEO Spiegel and CTO Murphy collectively own an astounding 335 million shares, representing 20% of shares outstanding and essentially 100% of the voting power.

I should note that CEO Spiegel receives rather modest compensation from the company.

The last several years have shown that management’s firm control of voting power has hurt shareholders due to the focus on spectacles instead of driving advertising profits higher, but one should not underestimate the power of large insider ownership at play here. With management explicitly signaling a commitment to profitable growth even prior to the announced involvement of this activist investor, I expect the profitability catalyst to come sooner than later.

SNAP Stock Key Risks

It is possible that SNAP is in the process of being disrupted by Meta Platforms and others. It is possible that the company is unable to drive GAAP margins higher as growth slows or even turns negative. It is possible that management does not engage with the activist or pursue any of the suggested ideas. It is possible that the stock does not perform well until management more urgently drives profitability higher.

SNAP Stock Conclusion

SNAP looks poised to see struggling top-line growth as META and others boost their dominance through AI. Yet my bullishness for the stock has grown largely due to the demolished-valuation. I expect the company to drive margin expansion which would directly address the relative discount to peers. SNAP is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I upgrade the stock to a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Transmission Channels Of The War On Iran To The Brazilian Economy
data.description:
We are now in the fifth week since the U.S. airstrike that killed top leaders of the Iranian regime, initiating a war involving the United States and Israel against the country. More than a month of mutual bombardments between Iran and Israel has ensued, extending to other Persian Gulf nations, U.S. military installations—and even Cyprus. From a global perspective, the impact has stemmed primarily from disruptions to regional production of goods and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

An impasse currently prevails, suggesting a prolongation and probable intensification of the war: while U.S. troops are converging for potential air and naval invasions—and Trump, on March 26, extended his deadline to bomb Iran’s power plants to April 6—the Iranians have vowed to assert sovereignty over the Strait and impose heavy tolls on traffic passing through it. It is no coincidence that Fatih Birol—head of the International Energy Agency (IEA)—stated that a war with Iran poses the greatest threat to global energy "in history."

The channels through which this war impacts the Brazilian economy are manifold. On March 27, the price of Brent crude oil briefly surpassed US$110 per barrel. While this development improves the country's terms of trade—given that it is now a net oil exporter (Figure 1)—this price surge has already begun to drive up domestic costs for gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel, thereby increasing transportation, logistics, and airfare costs and fueling inflation in the short term. Additionally, it is worth highlighting the country's dependence on imported fertilizers.

The OECD’s Interim Economic Outlook 2026 report, released this week, indicates a slight downward revision in its GDP growth projections for Brazil for 2026 and 2027—dropping from 1.7% to 1.5% this year and from 2.2% to 2.1% next year—compared to the report issued in December. The overall trade balance is expected to benefit from rising prices for crude oil exports. Conversely, it is worth noting the negative, albeit less significant, impact that freight and other costs will have on other components of the current account of the balance of payments.

Regarding trade with Middle Eastern countries affected by the war, Brazil exports goods amounting to approximately 0.6% of its GDP to the region, while imports account for roughly 0.3% of GDP. Certain specific sectors will suffer heavier impacts, such as corn exports, 25% of which are destined for the Middle East, according to Cassiana Fernandez, Mirella Mirandola Sampaio, and Vinicius Moreira. “Brazil: Economic Outlook after the Oil Shock,” JPMorgan, Global Economic Research, 19 March 2026. It is also important to highlight that 15% of Brazil's fertilizer imports originate from the war-affected region—fertilizers with properties that render them non-substitutable by those from other sources, such as those imported from Morocco.

Tax revenue is expected to rise, reflecting the increase in oil exports. The Ministry of Finance estimates that a Brent crude price of US$100 per barrel would result in an increase of nearly 1% of GDP in additional revenue relative to the 2026 budgetary projections.

At the same time—as in many other countries—fiscal measures have been implemented to mitigate the effects of rising diesel prices.
